source: ESV Study Bible (Crossway) — Used under license
book_intro: The letter to the Hebrews was written to encourage Christians in a time of trial. It does so by focusing on the absolute supremacy and sufficiency of Jesus Christ. While God spoke in the past "many times and in many ways," he has now spoken to us "by his Son," Jesus Christ, who is the "exact imprint" of God's nature and who "upholds the universe by the word of his power" (1:1-3). Jesus accomplished complete salvation for all who trust in him (1:1-10:18). We dare not "neglect such a great salvation" (2:3; 5:12-6:20; 10:19-39). Rather, in our faith and in our everyday living, we should imitate the example of Christ and of those on the honor roll of faith (chs. 11-13). This letter, whose author is unknown, was probably written between a.d. 60 and 70.
chapter_intro: The letter opens with a majestic declaration: God, who spoke through the prophets, has now spoken his final and definitive word through the Son. The chapter establishes Christ's supremacy over angels through a chain of seven Old Testament citations, showing that the Son holds a title, a throne, and a role no angel could ever claim.

passages.title: God's Final Word Through the Son
passages.text: The opening three verses are among the most compressed and exalted in all of Scripture. The Son is described as heir of all things, agent of creation, radiance of God's glory, and exact imprint of his nature — a cluster of terms that assert both his divine identity and his unique mediating role. The phrase 'upholds the universe by the word of his power' echoes Stoic cosmological language but redirects it: the sustainer of all things is not an impersonal logos but the Son who also accomplished purification for sins.

passages.title: Superior to the Angels
passages.text: Seven Old Testament quotations — drawn from Psalms, 2 Samuel, and Deuteronomy — demonstrate that no angel has ever been addressed as Son, invited to sit at God's right hand, or given a throne that lasts forever. Angels worship and serve; the Son reigns. The contrast sets the stage for the first warning: if the word spoken through angels (the Mosaic law, cf. Gal 3:19) demanded obedience, how much more does the word spoken through the Son?

notes.text: Long ago … many times and in many ways — The varied modes of OT revelation (visions, dreams, types, ceremonies) are now superseded by a single, definitive mode: the Son himself.
these last days — The eschatological era inaugurated by Christ's coming. The Son is the heir of all things, a reference to Ps 2:8 ('I will give you the nations as your inheritance').
exact imprint of his nature — The Greek charakter (stamp, impression) implies a perfect representation, not a copy. The Son shows us exactly what God is like.
You are my Son — Ps 2:7, cited here as applying to the exalted, enthroned Christ, not merely his eternal generation. The Father's declaration marks the Son's inauguration as messianic King.
Sit at my right hand — Ps 110:1, the most-quoted OT text in the NT. The posture of sitting — as opposed to angels who stand — signals completed, authoritative rule.
